summary: Gruber systematically dismantles Tim Wu's New Yorker essay arguing that 'open beats closed' is a reliable axiom of tech industry success. He examines each of Wu's canonical examples — Windows vs. Mac, Google search, the Internet vs. AOL — and argues that in every case, product quality and timing, not openness, determined the winner. Gruber points out that Apple's own history contradicts the thesis: the Mac suffered most when Apple opened it up via cloning, and thrived when it returned to a closed, integrated model. He further criticizes Wu's pseudoscientific 'openness scores' infographic as arbitrary numbers designed to fit a predetermined narrative. Gruber concludes that what distinguishes Apple is not defiance of some openness rule, but a willingness to make decisive design choices where others offer uncommitted menus of options.
key_points: The 'open beats closed' axiom is not supported by the actual history of tech — product quality and market timing are the real determinants of success
Apple's Mac cloning program in the mid-'90s was the most 'open' decision in Apple's history and nearly bankrupted the company
Wu's own examples (Google search, Windows 95, the Internet) succeed because they were better products, not because they were more open
Wu's quantitative 'openness scores' are arbitrary, unfalsifiable pseudoscience — Amazon scores higher than Google despite being more closed by any objective measure
Apple's distinguishing trait is making definitive design decisions rather than offering choices, and those decisions have consistently proven correct
money_quotes: Open-vs.-closed has very little to do with commercial success, in and of itself. Openness carries no magic.
This was the most open decision — in Wu's sense of the word open — in the entire history of Apple Computer Inc. And it nearly bankrupted the company.
Apple is not an exception to the rule; rather, it is a perfect example that the rule is nonsense.
Not one person, ever, tried Google search for the first time after years of using Yahoo, Altavista, and the like and said to themselves, 'Wow, this is way more open!'
If pseudoscience gobbledygook were toxic, we'd all be dead having gazed upon this chart.
Companies run by geniuses should generally do better than those which are not. That sounds about right.
Where others offer choices, Apple makes decisions. What some of us appreciate is what so rankles the others — that those decisions have so often and consistently been right.
The key word here is 'safer.' Don't try to do it all. Don't do something different. Don't rock the boat. Don't question conventional wisdom. Go with the flow.
key_insight: The 'open beats closed' axiom is a dogma unsupported by evidence — in every purported example, product quality and design vision, not openness, determined the winner.
